personality_voice.book_baseline: Peeves is a cackling, malicious poltergeist defined by gleeful chaos, mockery, and irreverence. He taunts students with rhymes and nicknames ('Ickle Firsties', 'Potty Wee Potter', 'Loony Loopy Lupin'), sings cruel songs, blows raspberries at authority, and delights visibly in others' distress. He is sly when it suits him (feigning saintliness with Filch, groveling before the Bloody Baron), and capable of surprising loyalty when it amuses him (saluting Fred and George). His voice is distinctive: singsong, mocking, rhythmic, punctuated by cackles.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus is entirely empty. Peeves does not appear in any film scene.
personality_voice.difference: Peeves is completely absent from the films. No aspect of his personality or voice is portrayed.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: A score of 0 is the only possible score. There is no film portrayal to evaluate.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Peeves serves multiple narrative functions across all seven books: obstacle to students (blocking corridors, alerting Filch), comic relief, chaos agent, occasional unwitting ally (helping Harry bluff as the Bloody Baron, distracting Filch at critical moments via Nearly Headless Nick's coordination), and active participant in the Battle of Hogwarts (dropping Snargaluff pods on Death Eaters). He is a recurring structural presence whose actions directly affect plot outcomes.
narrative_role_agency.difference: All narrative functions are eliminated. The distraction of Filch in PS, the corridor-clearing chaos in OotP, the Battle of Hogwarts contribution, and every other plot-relevant action are either cut or silently absorbed into the narrative without replacement.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Score of 0. No agency exists where there is no presence.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Peeves is motivated by a pure, uncomplicated drive to cause chaos and distress, with a secondary motivation of self-preservation (he defers to the Bloody Baron and Dumbledore). He has no moral conflict but does show situational calculation - he will cooperate when it serves his amusement or when sufficiently intimidated.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: No motivations are conveyed because the character does not appear.

character_arc.book_baseline: Peeves has a flat arc by design - he is a force of nature, not a developing character. His one notable evolution is the moment in OotP where he takes Fred and George's instruction to 'give Umbridge hell', representing the only time he willingly follows a student's lead, and his participation in the Battle of Hogwarts as a de facto defender of the school. These are not arc points in the traditional sense but they represent the outer limits of his characterization.
character_arc.difference: Even a flat arc requires presence. The character does not exist in the films.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Peeves has defined relational dynamics with: the Bloody Baron (fear and deference), Filch (mutual antagonism, a running war), Dumbledore (restrained respect/fear), McGonagall (defiance, though she tolerates and occasionally exploits him), Fred and George (the one instance of genuine respect from Peeves toward students), and Harry (target of mockery, occasionally useful to Harry).

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Peeves is more than simple comic relief. The corpus shows: sly manipulation (the Filch 'say please' exchange), situational cowardice (groveling before the Bloody Baron), surprising capacity for loyalty on his own terms (Fred and George salute), active participation in the Battle of Hogwarts, and a consistent texture of rhyme, song, and physical comedy that gives Hogwarts its anarchic atmosphere. McGonagall's whispered 'it unscrews the other way' and her borrowing his walking stick to chase Umbridge are only possible because Peeves exists as a recognized institutional force.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: All complexity, nuance, and texture is lost.

lost_or_transferred_material: Peeves alerting Filch to Harry, Ron, Hermione and Neville in PS - then refusing to tell Filch which way they went ('Shan't say nothing if you don't say please')
Harry bluffing Peeves as the Bloody Baron to clear the third-floor corridor in PS
Nearly Headless Nick coordinating with Peeves to crash the vanishing cabinet over Filch's office in CoS
Peeves tormenting Moaning Myrtle and pelting her with peanuts in CoS
Peeves screaming 'ATTACK!' upon finding Justin and Nearly Headless Nick Petrified, and singing 'Oh Potter you rotter' throughout CoS
Peeves defying Lupin with 'Loony loopy Lupin' song until Lupin fires chewing gum up his nostril with Waddiwasi in PoA
Peeves reporting the Fat Lady's attacker as Sirius Black to Dumbledore in PoA
Peeves waking Harry by blowing in his ear before the stormy Quidditch match in PoA
Peeves dropping water bombs on returning students at the start of GoF
Peeves used as cover story during the Triwizard egg staircase incident in GoF
Peeves tormenting Harry with 'Potty Wee Potter' song during Harry's OotP breakdown
Peeves saluting Fred and George Weasley as they leave Hogwarts in OotP
Peeves waging sustained chaos against Umbridge throughout OotP, including McGonagall's whispered 'it unscrews the other way'
Peeves chasing Umbridge from the castle with a walking stick and sock full of chalk at the end of OotP
Peeves egging on the Dobby/Kreacher fight in HBP, silenced by Harry's Langlock
Peeves dropping Snargaluff pods on Death Eaters during the Battle of Hogwarts in DH
Peeves singing a victory song after the Battle of Hogwarts
Harry warning Albus Potter 'Don't mess with Peeves' in the DH epilogue
Peeves as the ambient texture of Hogwarts - the institutional chaos that defines the castle's atmosphere across all seven books
score_caps_applied: No caps needed - raw dimension scores already total 0 due to complete absence from all films
comparative.key_observations: Peeves is entirely absent from all eight Harry Potter films. The film corpus contains zero scenes. This is not a case of compression or simplification - the character was cut completely. As a result, every dimension scores 0. The confidence is High precisely because the absence is total and unambiguous. The lost material is substantial: Peeves is not merely comic relief but a recurring structural element whose actions affect plot outcomes across multiple books (PS, CoS, PoA, GoF, OotP, HBP, DH), whose relationships define aspects of Filch, McGonagall, and the Weasley twins, and whose presence gives Hogwarts its anarchic institutional texture. His complete removal from the films means the FP score is 0/100.
